tweets.text:
From the Markets Update (https://t.co/URqvQfM9WQ):

Despite another (small) SPX gain, for a second session we had just 2 of 11 sectors higher according to CME Cash Indices, but again led by the heavyweight Tech sector +2.5% after +1.9% Friday (and +20% in May), again keeping the index afloat. The other was lightweight Energy +2.9%, only its 2nd gain in 8 sessions.

Six sectors though down around -1% more (after five Friday) led by Utilities -3%. Consumer Discretionary was -2.6%, and RE, Comm Services, Staples, and Health Care were also down more than -1% (all down at least -0.9% Friday).

tweets.text:
China's export prices suggest global inflation pressures are building:

China's overall export prices rose +5% YoY in April, the largest gain since April 2023.

This marks a sharp reversal from nearly 3 consecutive years of contraction.

This surge was driven by global commodities, including crude oil, metals, and semiconductors, amid the Iran War shock.

Mineral fuels led with a +22% YoY gain, while fertilizer prices rose +17% and electronic export prices soared +21% on severe chip shortages.

Global inflation is rebounding.
tweets.full_text:

Mineral fuels led with a +22% YoY gain, while fertilizer prices rose +17% and electronic export prices soared +21% on severe chip shortages.

Global inflation is rebounding.

tweets.text:
This is absolutely incredible.

Investors now perceive Nvidia to be as creditworthy as the US government.

Nvidia's $NVDA, 5-year credit default swap (CDS) is trading at ~38 basis points, slightly below the US sovereign CDS, at 40 basis points.

In other words, markets consider the world's largest company to be less likely to default on its obligations than the US federal government.

This comes as in FY2026, Nvidia carried only ~$8.5 billion in total debt against ~$10.6 billion in cash and generated nearly $100 billion in free cash flow, giving it one of the strongest balance sheets of any company in the world.

Even if Nvidia's earnings dropped -90%, it would still rank among the 100 most profitable companies in the world.

Markets are treating Nvidia as one of the safest companies on the planet.

tweets.text:
VenCap CIO David Clark says top 1% venture exits are 10x-ing:

"We always thought that the largest companies were going to continue to be an order of magnitude larger than we'd seen in prior cycles. And if anything, that's accelerating."

"Between 2020 and 2024, top 1% exits started at $10 billion. We updated those numbers in February this year, and a top 1% exit for '25 and the first two months of '26 was then $20 billion."

"We just updated them yesterday. And if you look at just the exits that have closed, it's now at $32 billion."

"And then if you think about OpenAI and Anthropic coming in, potentially we could be north of $100 billion by September... so we've 10x'd over the space of 24 months what a top 1% exit looks like."

@daveclark85 with @DavidGeorge83
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
AI Winners, Token Economics, and Venture Strategy w/ David Clark and David George

VenCap CIO David Clark joins a16z GP David George to discuss the AI supercycle and how it's changing the technology industry, including what bigger exits mean for venture capital, why we're not in https://t.co/eqk57cf0dX

tweets.text:
AI Winners, Token Economics, and Venture Strategy w/ David Clark and David George

VenCap CIO David Clark joins a16z GP David George to discuss the AI supercycle and how it's changing the technology industry, including what bigger exits mean for venture capital, why we're not in a bubble, why we're still early in enterprise AI adoption, and more.

00:00 Intro
00:38 The scale shift: Anthropic & OpenAI adding more revenue than hyperscalers
04:20 Skeuomorphic vs native AI applications in the enterprise
06:24 How the best AI companies run themselves differently
08:14 Top 1% exits 10x'd in 24 months
11:17 The half-life problem: Why 40% of AI leaders drop off every year
13:11 Token economics, cost pressure, and who captures value
17:00 Loss ratios, risk, and how to think about early stage
22:51 Are we in an AI bubble?
29:50 The future of venture capital in an AI world

@daveclark85 @DavidGeorge83

thread_tweets.text: If you were the dictator of Bitcoin, what would you do with the roughly 2.5 million coins presumed abandoned with exposed public keys?
thread_tweets.full_text: If you were the dictator of Bitcoin, what would you do with the roughly 2.5 million coins presumed abandoned with exposed public keys?

thread_tweets.text:
The experts on FYI gave different answers. @apruden08 and @dpuellARK would redistribute them to the end of the supply curve to strengthen miner incentives long-term. @nic_carter would leave them and hope America gets there first.

No clean consensus, which is exactly why the Bitcoin community has not resolved it. Watch the full episode.
https://t.co/zX7mAps96c

tweets.text:
Everyone's focused on layoffs and recession fears.

Since 1968, when continued unemployment claims were below their 78-week average, $1 invested in the S&P 500 grew to $43.97.

When claims were above their 78-week average, that same $1 grew to just $1.94.

Claims are currently below their 78-week average.

Read full analysis: https://t.co/FnMsV7Bsnm

thread_tweets.text:
Corporate profit margins just hit 20.6%, the highest on record, but the average worker keeps falling behind. 

It's not greed. It's not just Big Tech. 

The real cause has a name. 

Here's what's happening: 

1/
thread_tweets.full_text:
Corporate profit margins just hit 20.6%, the highest on record, but the average worker keeps falling behind. 

It's not greed. It's not just Big Tech. 

The real cause has a name. 

Here's what's happening: 

1/

thread_tweets.text:
Profits and paychecks come out of the same revenue pie.

From 1970 to 2000, worker pay was 64% of business revenue. 

Today it's 54%. 

A 10-point slice of the economy moved from wages to profits.

4/ https://t.co/zjXkMw9w2E
thread_tweets.full_text:
Profits and paychecks come out of the same revenue pie.

From 1970 to 2000, worker pay was 64% of business revenue. 

Today it's 54%. 

A 10-point slice of the economy moved from wages to profits.

4/ https://t.co/zjXkMw9w2E

thread_tweets.text:
The easy explanation is that a few trillion-dollar tech companies are dragging up the average. 

It's true, but it's still not the answer. 

The records are everywhere: manufacturing margins hit 15% in 2022 and sit near 13% today, the highest in 25 years. 

3/ https://t.co/MPzRhN0mDt

thread_tweets.text:
For a worker without much in stocks, all of it comes down to one thing: a paycheck that stopped keeping up.

Real private-sector income is now below its long-term trend, and the gap is widening.

That widening gap is the poor sentiment every consumer survey keeps picking up.

5/ https://t.co/K3Q9qYneB6

thread_tweets.text:
The proof shows it isn't greed or offshoring: the median firm saw no margin improvement at all. 

Only the superstar firms did. 

Margins even rose in services, where offshoring doesn't have a direct impact.

It's about market concentration. Everywhere.

8/
thread_tweets.full_text:
The proof shows it isn't greed or offshoring: the median firm saw no margin improvement at all. 

Only the superstar firms did. 

Margins even rose in services, where offshoring doesn't have a direct impact.

It's about market concentration. Everywhere.

8/

thread_tweets.text:
The labor share didn't fall because companies cut pay. 

It fell because the work moved to giant companies with more scale. 

Wages still rose, just slower than revenue. 

Pay shrank as a share of the pie, and profit took the difference.

7/
thread_tweets.full_text:
The labor share didn't fall because companies cut pay. 

It fell because the work moved to giant companies with more scale. 

Wages still rose, just slower than revenue. 

Pay shrank as a share of the pie, and profit took the difference.

7/

thread_tweets.text:
How did this happen?

A National Bureau of Economic Research paper "The Fall of the Labor Share and the Rise of Superstar Firms" helps with the answer.

It's not corporate greed. 

6/

Corporate profit margins just hit 20.6%, the highest on record, but the average worker keeps falling behind. 

It's not greed. It's not just Big Tech. 

The real cause has a name. 

Here's what's happening: 

1/

Profits and paychecks come out of the same revenue pie.

From 1970 to 2000, worker pay was 64% of business revenue. 

Today it's 54%. 

A 10-point slice of the economy moved from wages to profits.

4/ https://t.co/zjXkMw9w2E

The easy explanation is that a few trillion-dollar tech companies are dragging up the average. 

It's true, but it's still not the answer. 

The records are everywhere: manufacturing margins hit 15% in 2022 and sit near 13% today, the highest in 25 years. 

3/ https://t.co/MPzRhN0mDt

For a worker without much in stocks, all of it comes down to one thing: a paycheck that stopped keeping up.

Real private-sector income is now below its long-term trend, and the gap is widening.

That widening gap is the poor sentiment every consumer survey keeps picking up.

The proof shows it isn't greed or offshoring: the median firm saw no margin improvement at all. 

Only the superstar firms did. 

Margins even rose in services, where offshoring doesn't have a direct impact.

It's about market concentration. Everywhere.

8/

The labor share didn't fall because companies cut pay. 

It fell because the work moved to giant companies with more scale. 

Wages still rose, just slower than revenue. 

Pay shrank as a share of the pie, and profit took the difference.

7/

How did this happen?

A National Bureau of Economic Research paper "The Fall of the Labor Share and the Rise of Superstar Firms" helps with the answer.

It's not corporate greed. 

6/

More profits.

A foundation like that doesn't collapse randomly.
